turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Number 266. Sanks against the State of Georgia and others. Mr. Padnos. ORAL ARGUMENT BY MICHAEL D. PADNOS, ESO.

turns.name: PADNOS
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice and may it please the Court: The issue presented in the Slinks cases is quite different than the issue present in the previous case. The issue presented to the Court today is whether the State of Georgia may constitutionally creata a judicial procedure which,, by the terms of the statute, discriminates against all tenants and in favor of all property owners in the state. The issue is thus: whether a state can divide all its citisens under these two categories and discriminate in the matter presented in the statute. did involve, a money judgment. MR. JUSTICE STEWART; This is an action only for possession by the landlord and only upon certain limited ground; ; am I correct?

turns.name: PADNOS
turns.text: Well, the theory of the statute is only when a tenant is holding over may a landlord go into court unde:: this statute, but in fact, under any conditions. There is no

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well, he has to go into court and he has to take an oath, like a statement under oath in court; does he not?

turns.name: PADNOS
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: That what?

turns.name: PADNOS
turns.text: . He has to make a statement under oath that the tenant is either holding over or owes I believe its or owes money to the landlord.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Holding over beyond the lease. v MR. PADNOS; Holding over beyond the term of his lease hold. And as Judge Williams pointed out in the Lower Court, the tenants in this case are tenants at wiIlf and that's a good illustration of the problem with this statute. The entitled to a 60-day notice to vacat©; and theoretically the landlord can't do anything against them, or take any action to evict them until he has given them a 60-day notice. But» it doesn't work that way and what happens is the landlord can go in under any condition. Wow, as you point out, Mr, Justice Stewart, it certainly would be possible for a tenant later on to bring an action fdr false eviction, and indeed, the Georgia Supreme Cour makes the same suggestion, but that doesn’t do the tenant any Again, as you point out, the issue involved here is not money; the issue involved is possession and that's what the MR, JUSTICE HARLAN: Is this an old statute?

turns.name: PADIIOS
turns.text: The statute was first enacted in 1827, I think, and has been periodically modified since then, but It's a curious anacrohium. There just isn’t a statute in the country in which tenants are subjected to such a rigorous and immediate eviction and landlords are given such gracious privileges as the Georgia Eviction Statute. Indeed, in thinking about asking other people to be — to submit amicus briefs in the case, we wouldn't think of going anyplace else, because nobody else has a statute like thii And I think it' s important when we are talking about Lue process in the. Sniadach eaea, to make it clear that we lon't feel that Sniadach was an aberration of the Court’s blinking. Sniadach was in complete context with what the Court tas been doing for a lonci time, indeed, in Hoveyv, Elliott, igein, which v?as certainly years ago, the Court there also-took :he position that not only notice was necessary in 'order for Lue process, but also an opportunity to be heard. :he Sniadach case, the usual requisites of due process are recited about the procedure in that case, which did not afford

turns.name: PADNOS
turns.text: No, sir.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Do you have a separate equity court?

turns.name: PADNOS
turns.text: WE have a separate equity court, which also has other powers, but -—

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Have they then expressed this mechanism in terms of inherent judicial power? I suppose they must have had something like that in mind if they were accepting it.

turns.name: PADNOS
turns.text: X think they had something like that in mind „

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Or was it by stipulation of the parties# where no judicial discretion was involved? MR. PADNOS; 1 think the way it's worked here is that the judge has just issued an order so stating and saying that5s

turns.name: PADNOS
turns.text: Foregoing the bond. ,

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: But. not in the Supreme Court? HR. PADNOS: Nothing has gone to the Supreme Court. It may be improper to say so, but many people think that it’s an unconstitutional statute and have been giving us the benefit the doubt.on that question, so we have been' allowed to bring cases in the lowercourt and. the judges all the time just look down and say, "Wall, I shouldn't be doing this, but I'm going to let you file it anyway." That's about the way it has worked.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: That's the way equity developed in the first place? didn't it?

turns.name: PADNOS
turns.text: But we have excellent lower court Indeed, the Fulton County civil courts, until we began to present these cases, the Fulton Comity Civil Court attributed that to be future double rent, and m far as? 1 know, where the Legal Aid Society has not counseled in the cases, they may stil.' do so. The;y talc® a year's rent, because the court has just decided that siss months is the amount of time that these cases may last and therefore' they double that and they require every tenant to pay a year's rent.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: But your point would be the same whether it was a bond for single-; rent or quadruple rent.

turns.name: PADNOS
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: It’s the bond?

turns.name: PADNOS
turns.text: It's the bond? it's the fact that all tenants must pay it.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: So, there’s po denial of equal protection between the rich and the poor in terms of whether it’s single or double or quadruple?

turns.name: PADNOS
turns.text: Wall, it .COUld b®*

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, I nvsan not if you are an indigent.

turns.name: PADNOS
turns.text: Not if you are an indigent, it scarcely matters.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Which you claim to be? MR.PADNOS: Which we claim to be. MR. JUSTICE BRENNAN; Do you have a finding? specific finding of indigency. The issue that we present to the Court is the due process issue. It seems to me that on this ease there are so many different ways we can go; the statute is wrong for so many reasons? there are so many--defects in the statute. But, in orde: to present the simplest issue, I present to the Court the due process issue,&ich is just obviously wrong on other cases? on common sense? on decency and on the law. We could also argue the equal protection cases. We feel it's a clear violation of equal protection; equal protec™ tion between landlords and between tenants. I could get into the question of equal protection between.rich people and poor people, but I don't think wa ever need to reach that issue, because the statute violates the equal That, Your Honors, is the essence of" our case. MR. JUSTICE DOUGLAS; What does the Court mean on remedy. The next to the last paragraph on Page 42. MR. PADNOS; The Court there lists two very specific situations where we can go into equitys where there no land­ lord-tenant relationship, I believe is one of them. If the occupant is unable^ to post bond and the second one: if the landlord attempts to oust a tenant who is complying with the terms of his lease» But the equity courts don't want these cases and we find it very difficult to get into equity courts? because there is the separation of the jurisdiction. Landlord-tenant cases are civil court cases? at least in Pulton County, Every­ body recognises that and acts accordingly. The Superior Court/is our Court of Eqpifcy? doesn’t wish to have these cases and always says: "Go find your remedy in civil court? that’s where you belong." That's one of the other problems. I should also state that unlike Connecticut — I’m not too clear on the Connecticut situation -— the Georgia bond is not waivable. There is a Georgia Supreme Court opinion that judges have just sort of been clearing their throats and looking in a different direction and allowing landlords to waive the bond when they wish to come in. But, as a general rule they are not supposed to be able to do that. MR, JUSTICE WHITE: Is there anything in the Georgia Legislature —

turns.name: PADNOS
turns.text: yes? sir. The bill passed the Georgia Senate last year? and was up for the House on the last day of tie the session, but nothing earn© of it. I feel certain that the Legislature will act on it during 1972.

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: In this case —

turns.name: PADNOS
turns.text: Well, sir, my certainty of what the Georgia Legislature will do, I wouldn't like to rely on that.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Well, you seem to have an ambivalent position on that. You first suggested that you thought it would pass. I thought you were saying that in the nericfc session you thought ,it would pass.

turns.name: PADNOS
turns.text: Well, I think I was substituting hone for certainty. I certainly hone that we'll have a better law.
